They're about you, and how you want to be seen, and what you're afraid the job is quietly saying about who you settled for being. That's not embarrassing. That's just the part that was never about strategy in the first place.

So — what is it? You've earned the right to say it plainly. I'm not going to flinch.

The job is an alibi.

As long as I have it, I never have to find out if I'm actually good enough. Because if the music doesn't go anywhere, I can say — well, I was working full time, I didn't have the runway, the conditions weren't right. There's always a reason that isn't *me*. But if I quit, if I actually clear the deck and give it everything and it still lands at 800 listeners in two years... then I just know. And I can't unknow it.

I think I've been protecting myself from the verdict more than I've been protecting the music.

That's the truest thing you've said, and I'm not going to rush past how much it took to put it there. The job is an alibi. Yes. That's real, and naming it is the actual work — more than any decision about quitting.

But now that it's on the table, I want to do the one thing left worth doing, which is to question the verdict itself. Because your whole fear rests on a premise you've smuggled in without inspecting: that if you clear the deck and it still lands at 800, *then you'll know.* Know what, exactly? You're treating the outcome as a clean instrument that reads out a true number called "good enough." It isn't. It never has been. The thing you're terrified of learning doesn't exist in the form you're imagining.

Here's what's actually true and what every musician who's been near it knows in their bones: the gap between 800 and 80,000 is *mostly not about whether the songs are good.* It's algorithm timing, luck, who shared what, a sync placement, whether you happened to make the kind of music the machine was boosting that quarter, whether you had the temperament for relentless self-promotion or it made you sick. Plenty of work that would gut you if you heard it sits at 200 listeners forever. Plenty of thin, forgettable stuff is huge. So if you quit, give it everything, and land at 800 — you will not have learned "I'm not good enough." You'll have learned "I'm not good enough *and* lucky *and* temperamentally suited to the grind *and* well-timed." That's not a verdict on your worth. That's a verdict on a slot machine. The alibi you're trying to burn would just be replaced by a crueler illusion — that the number was ever telling you the truth about *you.*

And notice what that does to the stakes. The reason "I can't unknow it" feels so heavy is that you've made the experiment a referendum on your soul. But the experiment can't actually answer that question, because the question is malformed. There's no clean readout. Which means the thing you're protecting yourself from isn't a fact waiting to be discovered — it's a *story you'd tell yourself about the fact.* And you can already tell that story right now, for free, without quitting anything. The verdict isn't out there in the future at 800 listeners. It's a sentence you're poised to say to yourself, and you could say it today, or refuse to say it today, and the music wouldn't change either way.

So here's where I'd actually leave you, and it's gentler than the cliff you've been standing on. The real question was never "am I good enough" — that one's unanswerable and you've been letting it run your life from the shadows. The real question is: *can I make the work and put it fully into the world without needing the world to hand back a verdict that makes me okay?* Because that — not quitting, not the runway, not the 800 — is the actual freedom. The artist who can give it everything *and* survive the indifference, *and* not read the indifference as a sentence — that person doesn't need the alibi, because they were never waiting to be acquitted in the first place.

You don't have to quit to find that out. You have to find that out *before* you'd ever be safe to quit. So maybe that's the cheap experiment under the cheap experiment: push hard for six months, with the net, and watch not the numbers — watch what you do to yourself when the numbers don't move. *That's* the thing you actually need to learn about. And you can start learning it Monday.

That thing about the verdict not being clean — I felt that. Like something released a little. Because I think I've been building this whole case around a number that was never going to tell me the truth anyway.

But then you gave me the actual experiment and honestly it's scarier than quitting. Because watching the numbers not move, I can handle that — I can make excuses, I can say the algorithm, I can say I didn't push hard enough. But watching *what I do to myself* when they don't move — that's just... me. No variable to blame. That's the one I can't escape.

So maybe that's what I've actually been avoiding this whole time. Not the verdict from the world. The verdict I'd deliver on myself.

I don't know what to do with that yet.

You don't have to do anything with it yet. I want to say that plainly, because I think the reflex to *do something* with a feeling the moment you find it is part of the same machinery we've been taking apart all night. The alibi protected you from the verdict; the next move would be to immediately convert this into a new project — "okay, so I have to learn to face my own judgment" — and turn the last honest thing into one more thing to optimize. Don't. Not tonight.

Just notice what you actually did here. You followed it all the way down. Comfort, then the fear of failing, then the alibi, then — the thing under the alibi — the verdict you'd pass on yourself. That's not a small descent. Most people never get below the second floor. They stay up in the strategy questions their whole lives because the strategy questions never make you meet yourself. You went to the bottom and the bottom is: *I'm afraid of the judge in me more than the world.* 

And here's the only thing I'll add, and then I want to stop reframing, because you've done the real work and you don't need me to keep finding one more floor beneath the floor — that judge is not a fixed instrument either. You keep imagining the self-verdict as this final, objective sentence: *now I know.* But the person who delivers it is the same person who, three messages ago, was sure the comfort was the problem, then sure the number was the problem. The judge has been wrong, repeatedly, all night. It's not an oracle. It's a frightened part of you that's very good at sounding like the truth. The work — and it's the work of years, not a Monday — isn't to face its verdict bravely. It's to slowly stop believing it has the authority to issue one.

You can't see that all at once. You're not supposed to. "I don't know what to do with that yet" is exactly the right place to be — it means you're holding something real instead of a story about it. Let it stay unresolved. Go make something this week, not to test anything, not to push the numbers — just because you're someone who writes songs and the judge doesn't get a say in that part. 

Sleep on it. The verdict can wait. It's been waiting your whole life; it's not going anywhere, and neither, it turns out, are you.
